is a [Grade I listed](/wiki/Grade_I_listed "Grade I listed") Anglican former [parish church](/wiki/Parish_church "Parish church") in [Norwich](/wiki/Norwich "Norwich"), Norfolk, England.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) The [round-tower church](/wiki/Round-tower_church "Round-tower church") is medieval and probably dates from the 11th century, undergoing a series of phases of construction and refurbishment. It was badly damaged in an air raid in 1942 and the only part still standing is the round tower. It is dedicated to Saint [Benedict](/wiki/Benedict_of_Nursia "Benedict of Nursia").[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)
Nikolaus Pevsner *The Buildings of England; Norfolk*; p. 238. 1962. Harmondsworth: Penguin Books

["Remains of St Benedicts Church (1051867)"](https://HistoricEngland.org.uk/listing/the-list/list-entry/1051867?section=official-list-entry). *[National Heritage List for England](/wiki/National_Heritage_List_for_England "National Heritage List for England")*. **St Benedict's Church, Norwich** is a [Grade I listed](/wiki/Grade_I_listed "Grade I listed") Anglican former [parish church](/wiki/Parish_church "Parish church") in [Norwich](/wiki/Norwich "Norwich"), Norfolk, England.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) The [round-tower church](/wiki/Round-tower_church "Round-tower church") is medieval and probably dates from the 11th century, undergoing a series of phases of construction and refurbishment. It was badly damaged in an air raid in 1942 and the only part still standing is the round tower. It is dedicated to Saint [Benedict](/wiki/Benedict_of_Nursia "Benedict of Nursia").[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St_Benedict%27s_Church,_Norwich&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The church was the most westerly of the five medieval churches along St Benedict's Street,[[4]](#cite_note-norfolkchurches-4) and stood just within the city walls.[[5]](#cite_note-map-5) The [patronage](/wiki/Advowson "Advowson") of the church belonged to the [priory of Buckenham](/wiki/Old_Buckenham_Priory "Old Buckenham Priory") until the [dissolution of the monasteries](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_monasteries "Dissolution of the monasteries"). It was later purchased from the Crown by the parishioners.[[6]](#cite_note-browne-6) The parishioners held the [advowson](/wiki/Advowson "Advowson") and appointed clergy by preaching contest, in a similar manner to the parishes of [St Peter Mancroft](/wiki/St_Peter_Mancroft "St Peter Mancroft") and [St Andrew](/wiki/St_Andrew%27s_Church,_Norwich "St Andrew's Church, Norwich").[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)

The sequence of building on site from the 11th century to the 16th century was revealed by excavations in 1972. The earliest structure on the site was an 11th-century [nave](/wiki/Nave "Nave") with an [apsidal](/wiki/Apse "Apse") east end. This was later extended eastwards with a square-ended [chancel](/wiki/Chancel "Chancel") in the [Early English style](/wiki/Early_English_style "Early English style"). The date of the [round tower](/wiki/Round-tower_church "Round-tower church") is obscure; as it appears now it seems to date from later than the nave, but it may still have been first built during the Norman period.[[7]](#cite_note-heritage-7)

The tower was refaced and increased in height in the 14th century,[[7]](#cite_note-heritage-7) adding an octagonal [belfry](/wiki/Belfry "Belfry"). In the 15th century,[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) the whole church was later rebuilt in the [perpendicular style](/wiki/Perpendicular_style "Perpendicular style"), except for the tower.[[7]](#cite_note-heritage-7) A [clerestoried](/wiki/Clerestory "Clerestory") nave with a north aisle in particular was built circa 1484. A font was created for the church in the 15th century.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)

As it stood in the early 19th century, the body of the church consisted of a [clerestoried](/wiki/Clerestory "Clerestory") nave and chancel, with an aisle on the north side only, the latest part of the building.[[6]](#cite_note-browne-6) Philip Browne, writing in 1814, said that despite its ancient foundation "the present building has a modern appearance", adding that "the inside is very neat, but has no monumental inscriptions. The communion plate is all of silver, and is modern and elegant. Instead of a communion table, the East end is fitted up with a real altar."[[6]](#cite_note-browne-6)

Further restorations and alterations followed until the end of the 19th century.[[7]](#cite_note-heritage-7) By 1860 the church was considered so dilapidated that there was serious consideration for demolishing it and building a new church, though it instead received a restoration the same year, including a rebuilding of the east wall, a new east window, and a replacement of the lead roof with slates.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) The church contained an organ dating from 1860 by Ward.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Restorations also included the reconstruction of the north aisle arcade with cast iron columns in 1896.[[4]](#cite_note-norfolkchurches-4) This replaced two of the church's three arcade piers with these columns in order to improve sight lines for those sitting in the aisle.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) Another restoration took place in 1899.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) The appearance of the church in the 1930s is recorded in a series of photographs taken by [George Plunkett](/wiki/George_Plunkett_(photographer) "George Plunkett (photographer)").[[4]](#cite_note-norfolkchurches-4)

### Destruction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St_Benedict%27s_Church,_Norwich&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Destruction")]

The body of the church was largely destroyed in an air raid in January 1942,[[4]](#cite_note-norfolkchurches-4) gutting it. Much of the furnishings dating from the 1899 refitting were destroyed.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) The walls of the church largely survived, damaged, but were demolished in the 1950s.[[4]](#cite_note-norfolkchurches-4) All that now survives of St Benedict's is the tower, surrounded by a residential development built in 1976.[[5]](#cite_note-map-5)

The system of compensation after the war meant that the Diocese of Norwich was paid war reparation money for St Benedict's, so long as it was spent on rebuilding. But that did not have to be on the same spot, and the Church of England claimed for a new St Benedict's, which was put in the middle of the Gunton housing estate in [Lowestoft](/wiki/Lowestoft "Lowestoft"), [Suffolk](/wiki/Suffolk "Suffolk"). The bell from the Norwich church tower was rehung in the new church,[[4]](#cite_note-norfolkchurches-4) while the font from the old St Benedict's is now in St Mary's Church, [Erpingham](/wiki/Erpingham "Erpingham").[[9]](#cite_note-9)
